(born July 18, 1947)[[2]](#cite_note-brachman-2)[[3]](#cite_note-amg-3) is an American former actress and theater director. She began her career as a stage actress, starring in a [Broadway](/wiki/Broadway_theatre "Broadway theatre") production of [Neil Simon](/wiki/Neil_Simon "Neil Simon")'s *[The Gingerbread Lady](/wiki/The_Gingerbread_Lady "The Gingerbread Lady")* (1970–1971), for which she won a [Theatre World Award](/wiki/Theatre_World_Award "Theatre World Award").

She began her career as a stage actress, starring in a [Broadway](/wiki/Broadway_theatre "Broadway theatre") production of [Neil Simon](/wiki/Neil_Simon "Neil Simon")'s *[The Gingerbread Lady](/wiki/The_Gingerbread_Lady "The Gingerbread Lady")* (1970–1971), for which she won a [Theatre World Award](/wiki/Theatre_World_Award "Theatre World Award").

American actress

**Ayn Ruymen** (born July 18, 1947)[[2]](#cite_note-brachman-2)[[3]](#cite_note-amg-3) is an American former actress and theater director. She began her career as a stage actress, starring in a [Broadway](/wiki/Broadway_theatre "Broadway theatre") production of [Neil Simon](/wiki/Neil_Simon "Neil Simon")'s *[The Gingerbread Lady](/wiki/The_Gingerbread_Lady "The Gingerbread Lady")* (1970–1971), for which she won a [Theatre World Award](/wiki/Theatre_World_Award "Theatre World Award").

After relocating to Los Angeles, Ruymen made her feature film debut in [Paul Bartel](/wiki/Paul_Bartel "Paul Bartel")'s horror film *[Private Parts](/wiki/Private_Parts_(1972_film) "Private Parts (1972 film)")* (1972) and appeared in several television films during the 1970s. She had a lead role on the [sitcom](/wiki/Sitcom "Sitcom") *[The McLean Stevenson Show](/wiki/The_McLean_Stevenson_Show "The McLean Stevenson Show")* from 1976 to 1977. She made her last screen appearance in the television film *Firestorm: 72 Hours in Oakland* (1993). Since then, Ruymen has worked primarily as a theater director of productions for the [Mendocino](/wiki/Mendocino,_California "Mendocino, California") Theatre Company.

## Early life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ayn_Ruymen&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early life")]

Ruymen was born July 18, 1947, in [Brooklyn](/wiki/Brooklyn "Brooklyn"), New York City, one of six children, and was raised in [Long Island](/wiki/Long_Island "Long Island").[[2]](#cite_note-brachman-2) Her father, George Ruymen, worked as a building inspector for the City of New York.[[4]](#cite_note-4) She began working as an actress while still a teenager, performing in theater productions in [New Jersey](/wiki/New_Jersey "New Jersey").[[2]](#cite_note-brachman-2) In 1969, Ruymen appeared as a model at the [Miami International Boat Show](/wiki/Miami_International_Boat_Show "Miami International Boat Show") to promote Sungard [sunscreen](/wiki/Sunscreen "Sunscreen"), covering half her face in it before spending time in the sun, demonstrating the product's efficacy at preventing [sunburns](/wiki/Sunburn "Sunburn").[[5]](#cite_note-5)

## Career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ayn_Ruymen&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Career")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8e/Ayn_Ruymen_%281977%29_crop.jpg/250px-Ayn_Ruymen_%281977%29_crop.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ayn_Ruymen_(1977)_crop.jpg)

Ruymen in *[The McLean Stevenson Show](/wiki/The_McLean_Stevenson_Show "The McLean Stevenson Show")*, 1977

In 1970, she was cast as Polly Meara in a [Broadway](/wiki/Broadway_theatre "Broadway theatre") production of [Neil Simon](/wiki/Neil_Simon "Neil Simon")'s *[The Gingerbread Lady](/wiki/The_Gingerbread_Lady "The Gingerbread Lady")* opposite [Maureen Stapleton](/wiki/Maureen_Stapleton "Maureen Stapleton"), which ran between December 1970 and 1971.[[6]](#cite_note-ibdb-6) Critic [George Oppenheimer](/wiki/George_Oppenheimer "George Oppenheimer") praised Ruymen for "showing great promise" in the "difficult" role.[[7]](#cite_note-7) For her performance, she won the [Theatre World Award](/wiki/Theatre_World_Award "Theatre World Award") in 1971.[[8]](#cite_note-pb-8) During her stage career, she became a member of [Actors' Equity](/wiki/Actors%27_Equity_Association "Actors' Equity Association").[[2]](#cite_note-brachman-2)

Ruymen subsequently relocated to [Los Angeles](/wiki/Los_Angeles "Los Angeles") in late 1971 to pursue a film career.[[2]](#cite_note-brachman-2) She commented that she initially disliked California: "I found the [Sunset Strip](/wiki/Sunset_Strip "Sunset Strip") disgusting. I was trying to get work and nothing happened for six months. It makes you want to pull your hair out."[[2]](#cite_note-brachman-2) In early 1972, she was cast in an episode of the medical drama series *[Medical Center](/wiki/Medical_Center_(TV_series) "Medical Center (TV series)")*.[[2]](#cite_note-brachman-2) She was subsequently cast in the lead role of [Paul Bartel](/wiki/Paul_Bartel "Paul Bartel")'s horror film *[Private Parts](/wiki/Private_Parts_(1972_film) "Private Parts (1972 film)")* (1972), playing a young woman who uncovers dark secrets in a Los Angeles hotel operated by her aunt. The following year, she appeared in the television film *[Go Ask Alice](/wiki/Go_Ask_Alice_(film) "Go Ask Alice (film)")*.[[9]](#cite_note-mf-9) She later had a minor uncredited role as a nurse in [Steven Spielberg](/wiki/Steven_Spielberg "Steven Spielberg")'s *[Jaws](/wiki/Jaws_(film) "Jaws (film)")* (1975).[[9]](#cite_note-mf-9)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6e/Barbara_Stuart%2C_Ayn_Ruymen%2C_and_McLean_Stevenson_%281977%29.jpg/250px-Barbara_Stuart%2C_Ayn_Ruymen%2C_and_McLean_Stevenson_%281977%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Barbara_Stuart,_Ayn_Ruymen,_and_McLean_Stevenson_(1977).jpg)

[Barbara Stuart](/wiki/Barbara_Stuart "Barbara Stuart"), Ruymen and [McLean Stevenson](/wiki/McLean_Stevenson "McLean Stevenson") in a publicity still for [The McLean Stevenson Show](/wiki/The_McLean_Stevenson_Show "The McLean Stevenson Show") 1977

Between 1976 and 1977, Ruymen had a lead role on the [sitcom](/wiki/Sitcom "Sitcom") *[The McLean Stevenson Show](/wiki/The_McLean_Stevenson_Show "The McLean Stevenson Show")*, playing the daughter of a hardware store proprietor.[[10]](#cite_note-mclean-10)

Ruymen's last film appearance was the 1993 television film *Firestorm: 72 Hours in Oakland*. In September 2009, she married Robert Ross, a visual artist and instructor at the Mendocino Arts Center and [Oregon School of Arts & Crafts](/wiki/Oregon_School_of_Arts_%26_Crafts "Oregon School of Arts & Crafts").[[1]](#cite_note-reed-1)[[11]](#cite_note-11)

Ruymen has directed numerous plays for the [Mendocino](/wiki/Mendocino,_California "Mendocino, California") Theatre Company (MTC) as early as 1993, when she directed a production of *[Love Letters](/wiki/Love_Letters_(play) "Love Letters (play)")*, which featured a nightly rotating cast.[[12]](#cite_note-12)

In 1996, she directed 'A Perfect Ganesh' by Terrence McNally.

In August 2009, she directed an MTC stage production of [W. Somerset Maugham](/wiki/W._Somerset_Maugham "W. Somerset Maugham")'s *The Circle*.[[13]](#cite_note-13)
